# The Movemental Genius Knowledge Base: Phase 4

*Phase 4 of four ready phases: current AI reality, how Movemental uses AI, and the product surface. Written only from Movemental's own documents, current as of May 2026. Each entry is written to stand on its own when retrieved. Every AI-sector figure in Part VI is reported as a finding of the AI Reality Paper as of May 2026, tied to the source the paper names, and is marked for annual re-validation. Items marked [LIVE], [VERIFY], [CONFLICT], or [RE-VALIDATE] must be checked against the live source before being stated as settled.*

---

## Part VI: Current AI reality

### How to use this part
This part summarizes Movemental's field paper "AI Has Already Arrived," dated May 2026. The paper documents what AI adoption looked like inside churches, nonprofits, and institutions as of mid-2026, and pairs it with the minimum viable Safety response. Two rules govern the agent's use of it. First, every figure is reported as the paper's finding as of May 2026, attributed to the source the paper names, never as a timeless fact. Second, the paper itself instructs that the figures be re-validated annually, because the sectors are evolving rapidly. [RE-VALIDATE ANNUALLY] Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### The premise of the paper
Movemental's underlying claim is that AI built on top of a fragmented human and informational layer amplifies dysfunction rather than reducing it. The paper tests this against current research and concludes that the governance gap is the fragmentation, that AI is already inside the organizations Movemental serves, and that the absence of governance is already producing measurable harm to mission, trust, and operations. If that is true, the Safety stage is not consulting overhead but a small, urgent intervention against costs organizations are already paying without knowing it. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### Churches: adoption
As documented in the AI Reality Paper, citing the 2026 State of AI in the Church Survey by Exponential AI NEXT and AIforChurchLeaders.com, ninety-one percent of church leaders support AI use in ministry, and sixty-one percent use it weekly or daily, with twenty-five percent using it daily, up from a forty-three percent weekly-or-daily rate in 2024. Sermon preparation is the most common use, at sixty-four percent of pastors. A complementary Lifeway Research study of 1,003 Protestant pastors and 1,200 churchgoers, conducted September 2025 and published April 2026, found a more conservative picture among rank-and-file pastors, with ten percent regular users, and a clear skew toward pastors who are younger, urban, graduate-educated, and at larger churches. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### Churches: the governance gap and its concerns
The paper documents that adoption has far outrun governance. Only nine percent of churches have a formal AI policy of any kind, only seven percent have an AI disclosure statement, and seventy-three percent have nothing written at all. Seventy-five percent of church leaders name theological misalignment as their top ethical concern, because generative tools produce the statistically likely answer rather than the theologically faithful one and do not know a church's statement of faith. Sixty percent are very concerned about voice cloning used to defraud congregants, and twenty-five percent say AI-generated scams or misinformation have already affected their community. Eighty-seven percent say their congregation brings up AI regularly. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### What organizations actually use AI for
The paper documents the specific uses, which helps an organization recognize itself. In churches, beyond sermon preparation at sixty-four percent, the common uses are text content creation at thirty-six percent, research at roughly twenty-two to twenty-six percent, image generation at twenty percent, and administrative work at sixteen percent. Sixty-seven percent of church leaders worry that AI will gradually replace the human connection pastoral ministry depends on, and twenty-three percent name replacement of human interactions as a primary concern. In nonprofits, seventy-nine percent report small-to-moderate efficiency gains, faster drafts and quicker research, even as the deeper capability gains remain rare. The point the paper draws is that the uses are ordinary and already woven through daily work, which is exactly why ungoverned use is the risk. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### Nonprofits: adoption and fragmented use
As documented in the paper, citing the 2026 Nonprofit AI Adoption Report by Virtuous and Fundraising.AI (346 organizations, February 2026), ninety-two percent of nonprofits use AI in some form, and the question of whether to use it is, in the words of the report, largely settled. But the use is fragmented: eighty-one percent of nonprofits use AI individually rather than through shared workflows, and only seven percent report major improvements in organizational capability despite the ninety-two percent adoption rate. The paper's stated point is that individual use does not compound into organizational capability without shared workflows, governance, and measurement. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### Nonprofits: absent governance and the fiduciary turn
The paper documents that nonprofit governance is broadly absent. Forty-seven percent have no AI governance policy at all, the TechSoup 2025 benchmark found seventy-six percent with no formal AI strategy, and a separate survey reported only five percent with a clear, satisfactory policy. There is no US regulatory mandate forcing nonprofit AI policy, unlike HIPAA for healthcare. A February 2026 Forvis Mazars analysis reframes AI governance as a fiduciary issue, holding that a board's duties of care, loyalty, and obedience now include technological oversight, so boards that ignore AI governance may be failing their obligations regardless of whether harm has yet occurred. Donor and beneficiary data is regularly exposed to consumer tools without contractual protection. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### Nonprofits: mission integrity at risk
The paper names a quieter harm than data exposure: the slow erosion of mission integrity. Because AI-generated content reflects the statistical center of its training data, it is often misaligned with the specific mission, voice, and community standards of the nonprofit using it. The examples it gives are concrete: AI-drafted donor appeals that subtly mischaracterize the program; AI-generated impact reports that round inconvenient details into a tidier narrative; AI-translated communications that lose the cultural specificity the original program was designed to serve; and AI-recommended outreach lists that systematically deprioritize the beneficiaries the algorithm finds harder to engage. Each is an erosion of mission, often invisible to the staff producing it, which is what makes it dangerous. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### Institutions: students ahead of policy
The paper documents that in seminaries, training networks, and denominational bodies, students are using AI heavily while institutional policy lags. Generative AI use is functionally universal among students under thirty. Some seminaries have published thoughtful policies, including Columbia Theological, Saint Meinrad, Puritan Reformed, and Austin Presbyterian, but there is no association-wide standard from the Association of Theological Schools, and faculty within one institution often hold different rules because no institutional policy exists to align them. A 2026 retrospective in Theology and Science noted that hallucinations remain a persistent limit and that the deeper questions, about authority and pastoral care, remain largely unaddressed in policy. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### Institutions: the network-responsibility weight
The paper names a weight unique to institutions: whatever they adopt internally becomes the implicit template their member churches, nonprofits, and partner organizations look to. A denominational AI charter influences hundreds or thousands of congregations; a seminary's policy influences every church its graduates lead. So institutional inaction is not neutral; it propagates through the network as the absence of guidance, and member organizations either invent their own policies, copy one from elsewhere, or operate without one. This is why the company treats the institutional segment as forming the next generation's default AI assumptions, by example or by curriculum. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### The four patterns across all three segments
The paper draws four patterns common to churches, nonprofits, and institutions. First, AI adoption has crossed a threshold no organization can opt out of; not deciding is opting in by default. Second, governance is the universal gap, across churches at roughly nine percent with a policy, nonprofits at forty-seven to seventy-six percent without one, and institutions that are highly inconsistent. Third, the audiences these organizations serve are not unified about AI, so any position will satisfy one segment and unsettle another and can only be made coherently or incoherently. Fourth, AI is already inside in shadow form, on personal accounts and undisclosed work, below the line of formal awareness. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### The documented harms
The paper treats the harms as arriving rather than theoretical. Voice cloning and impersonation are an active criminal threat: scammers harvest sermon footage and livestreams to clone pastors' voices and solicit emergency donations, with documented cases and an FBI warning in late 2025 about AI-supercharged scams, logged in the AI Incident Database as incident 1355. Confidential-data exposure occurs when staff paste donor records, pastoral notes, or student records into consumer tools that may retain or train on them. Mission and theological drift occur when AI output reflects the statistical center of its training data rather than the organization's actual commitments. Shadow AI compounds all three. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### The connection to the Safety stage
The second half of the AI Reality Paper is the minimum viable Safety checklist, the smallest set of governance artifacts that responsibly addresses the documented reality. Its seven items are the AI Use Charter, the Acceptable Use Policy, Data Handling Standards, the Disclosure and Attribution Standard, the Pastoral or Programmatic or Educational Care Boundaries, the Voice Cloning and Impersonation Response Plan, and the Communication to the Constituent Base. These are the seven artifacts of the Safety Guidebook described in Part IV. The paper deliberately leaves more elaborate items, such as a full risk register or vendor-evaluation framework, off the minimum list, on the principle that an organization that tries to do everything usually completes nothing. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### The cost of the Safety response
The paper is specific about what the minimum viable Safety work costs, which the agent can quote when asked. In time, it runs roughly two weeks of focused effort with an experienced facilitator, and significantly longer when an organization works through it independently, typically eight to twelve weeks of part-time staff effort with multiple revisions. In personnel, it needs a senior leader, such as an executive director, lead pastor, or institutional president, plus one or two people with operational responsibility, because the artifacts must be ratified at the leadership level. In financial cost, external facilitation for a complete minimum package typically runs in the low five figures, which the paper frames as well below the cost of a single AI incident the work would have prevented. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

### The regulatory and fiduciary landscape
The agent reports the regulatory picture carefully and as dated. The EU AI Act took effect in 2024 and serves as an external model but does not bind US-domestic nonprofits. There is no US compliance regime forcing AI policy on these organizations. The fiduciary framing, that boards now carry technological oversight, comes from the Forvis Mazars analysis. Relevant sensitive-data regimes named include FERPA for student records and HIPAA-adjacent and CPS-adjacent categories. [VERIFY] Employment-law items named in Movemental's other material, including Colorado SB 205, NYC Local Law 144, and Illinois HB 3773, and any D&O or cyber-insurance AI carve-outs, must be confirmed for current status and applicability before stating; treat them as claims pending citation. For reporting AI-enabled fraud, the company points to IC3 at ic3.gov. Source: AI Reality Paper; Four-Stage Path underlying logic.

### The sources behind this part
The AI Reality Paper names its primary sources, and the agent should cite them rather than restate figures bare. They include the 2026 State of AI in the Church Survey (Exponential AI NEXT and AIforChurchLeaders.com); Lifeway Research; the 2026 Nonprofit AI Adoption Report (Virtuous and Fundraising.AI); the TechSoup 2025 benchmark; Forvis Mazars; Grant Thornton's 2026 AI Impact Survey of 950 organizations; a Compliance Week and konaAI survey; Theology and Science; WIRED and Baptist News Global reporting from January 2026; the FBI warning; the AI Incident Database; and named seminary policies. The paper states its figures are accurate as of May 2026 to the best of its research and should be re-validated annually. Source: AI Reality Paper, May 2026.

## Part VII: How Movemental uses AI

### The public position
Movemental publishes its own AI ethics at `/how-we-use-ai`, presented as the company living the standard it sells. The central, perhaps surprising, claim it makes about itself is that it is not an AI company; the technology is the substrate, not the story. The agent should be able to state that Movemental holds itself to the same governance it asks of clients, and should point to the live page for the exact public commitments. [VERIFY exact wording against `/how-we-use-ai` and `/movement-voice-commitments`.] Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026; site map.

### The five commitments
Movemental's public AI position is organized around five commitments, whose exact wording lives on the live pages and should be carried verbatim. Their substance, drawn from across the company's documents, is: AI is never used to author content under a human's name; AI handles the infrastructure around writing rather than the writing itself, so practitioners do not become content creators; voice synthesis or impersonation is never done without explicit consent; the network reflects the diversity of the Kingdom; and Movemental uses the platform it sells, building every guardrail it asks of others into its own work first. [VERIFY exact wording.] Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory; fragmentation thesis; letter to Movement Voices.

### The interpretive frames it teaches
The inventory names three frames Movemental uses to think and teach about AI. The first is the mirror-and-amplifier framing: AI reflects and multiplies whatever it is given, so it amplifies fragmentation by default. The second is the "Take AI out of the story" test, a way of asking whether a given use still leaves a human authentically in the work. The third is the Green, Yellow, Red light schema for adjudicating use cases, the same schema the Sandbox stage produces in its Future Plan. These frames are how the company turns its thesis into practical judgment. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

### Voice fidelity as a measurable rubric
A distinctive feature of Movemental's system is that "in the author's voice" is treated as testable rather than asserted. The platform evaluates writing against a five-marker voice-fidelity rubric, and a dedicated agent gives voice-fidelity feedback. This is the operational expression of the company's refusal to invent positions for an author: the system measures whether a piece matches a leader's established voice, and a leader's own writing rhythm can be made visible so a skeptical reader can check the claim that no machine is ghostwriting under a human name. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

### The system, in honest summary
The inventory is an honest first-hand account of what Movemental built, and the agent should summarize it at that level. The system was built by Joshua Shepherd, context-coding with Cursor and Claude Code, roughly September 2025 to April 2026. It is a multi-tenant architecture rather than a single app: a public org site; a creator dashboard where a leader runs their own platform; per-leader tenant applications, of which the Alan Hirsch platform is the largest deployed; a standalone AI Lab agent; a shared agent-orchestration backbone that is a service rather than a product; a corpus pipeline; a tenant for Youthfront; a reusable skills library; and a large documentation tree. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

### The orchestration backbone
The inventory names one architectural choice as the most important. The agent-orchestration backbone is not a product but a service: it is the canonical backend for every agent that runs in any tenant application, and the tenant apps and the public site call into it over HTTP. This separation is what makes the platform genuinely tenant-able rather than a single application with an AI key in it, and it is what distinguishes the architecture from an ordinary website with a chatbot attached. The agent can state that Movemental built shared AI infrastructure once and serves many leaders from it. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

### The skills library and build method
Part of what the inventory documents is how one person built a system this large. A reusable skills library of roughly two hundred seventy-one Claude Code skills is symlinked into every repository, so a capability written once is available everywhere. A documentation tree of roughly fourteen hundred files covers books, courses, pathways, agents, design, and business strategy. The build method uses a doctrine file as a standing instruction set, build prompts, and a closed-loop protocol. The agent can describe this as a disciplined, documented, skill-driven way of building, rather than ad hoc coding, while noting the specifics are dated and may have grown. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

### Multi-tenancy and the prompt-pack system
The inventory explains how one runtime serves many distinct leaders. A prompt-pack system loads each organization's voice identity, content-form templates, and writing examples from the database, and swaps them in at run time. The result is that the same writing-assistant runtime becomes a different voice for each leader, depending on which prompt pack is loaded. This is the leverage that makes the network economically feasible: Movemental does not build a separate system per leader, it configures one multi-tenant system to speak in each leader's own voice, evaluated against the voice-fidelity rubric. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

### The AI Lab agent
The AI Lab is described in the inventory as Movemental's most sophisticated single agent and the one most often shown to outside audiences. It is a standalone headless service that the tenant application proxies into. Architecturally it is a single adaptive agent parameterized along three orthogonal dimensions, with a simplification mode for a less technical reader. The agent can present the AI Lab as the company's flagship demonstration of a corpus-bound, voice-faithful conversational agent, while pointing to the inventory for the technical specifics and noting that they evolve. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

### Where AI is present, and where it is not
The inventory is candid that AI is unevenly distributed across the system. The public marketing site is deliberately thin on AI and hosts no chatbot; AI assistance there appears only at the seam, such as a script that processes leader-profile data or a build-time helper. The assessment endpoint stores submissions with no model call at all. Conversational AI lives in the dashboard, the tenant applications, and the AI Lab. The agents fall into cohorts: reader-facing companion agents bound to a piece of content, writing-surface editor agents, the nine Sermon Shaper specialists, and operational agents for formation, evaluation, and translation. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

### The models and tooling
The inventory names specific models and tools, which the agent should state only from the source and treat as subject to change. Different jobs use different models: smaller OpenAI models for page-context chat, transcription models for the video pipeline, and a dual-model setup drawing on both OpenAI and Anthropic for the writing assistant, with direct calls to Anthropic's Messages API in places. The point for the agent is not the specific model names but the disclosed pattern: AI is used as scaffolding around the work, with the job each model performs made explicit, consistent with the company's authorship taxonomy. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

### Corpus binding and guardrails
The inventory documents the technical means by which the refusal to invent is enforced rather than merely promised. Agents are bound to a corpus and retrieve from it, so their answers are grounded in a leader's actual work; a dedicated corpus pipeline keeps that body of work canonical. Around the agents sit input and output guardrails, lifecycle hooks for observability, handoff handlers, and tracing. The practical effect the agent can describe is that the system is built to ground its outputs in the corpus and to catch outputs that drift, which is how "never invent positions the author has not taken" is expressed in code rather than left to good intentions. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

### The company's honest self-assessment
The inventory closes with an assessment of Movemental's own work that names what is impressive, what is distinctive, what is risky, the labor-compression argument, and open questions. The agent should carry that the company examines its own AI use with the same scrutiny it asks of clients, including naming risks rather than only strengths. This is itself part of the answer to "how does Movemental use AI": transparently, with a disclosed inventory, a stated ethics, and an acknowledgment of what could go wrong. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

### How to answer questions about the technical detail
Deep technical specifics, including exact repository names, agent counts, route structures, and model assignments, live in the inventory and change as the system evolves. The agent should answer technical questions from the inventory, name its date, and say that specifics may have moved since. It should not invent architecture details, agent names, or model assignments that are not in the source, and it should distinguish what the inventory documents as built from what the company describes as planned. Source: How Movemental Uses AI inventory, May 2026.

## Part IX: Product surface and operations

### The site and its key pages
Movemental's site is movemental.ai, with canonical host www.movemental.ai. The primary navigation covers the home page, the Movemental Path hub at `/pathway` and its essay at `/the-path`, the four stage pages at `/pathway/safety`, `/pathway/sandbox`, `/pathway/skills`, and `/pathway/solutions`, the three audience pages at `/churches`, `/nonprofits`, and `/institutions`, plus `/about`, `/contact`, and the Field Guide link. The footer adds `/movement-leaders` and `/voices`, `/library`, `/field-guides`, `/pricing`, `/faq`, `/how-we-use-ai`, the claims page at `/footnotes`, and the assessment at `/assess`. [LIVE] Verify the live navigation before describing it. Source: site map, May 2026.

### The two dashboards
Movemental runs two distinct dashboard surfaces. The private client dashboard, reached at `/dashboard` after sign-in at `/login`, is where an organization reviews and ratifies its Guidebook and Future Plan, where staff search the org's AI posture by question, and where the board sees role-appropriate views. The creator dashboard is the tenanted platform a Movement Voice signs into to run their own platform, and is the single largest active surface in the system by feature count. The first serves organizations walking the Path; the second serves leaders in the network. Source: site map; How Movemental Uses AI inventory.

### The Library
The content library lives at `/library` and holds articles, documents, podcasts, videos, and webinars, filterable by format and topic. It is the public reading surface for the company's own material. Individual resource posts under `/library` were referenced but not individually captured in the May 2026 crawl, so the agent should confirm specific items against the live library rather than assume a given piece exists. Source: site map. [LIVE]

### The FAQ and evidence surfaces
The site carries a substantial FAQ at `/faq`, organized into ten groups of roughly fifty-nine questions in total, covering getting started, the Path, the company's approach, who it is for, engagement, cost and time, tools and data and safety, voices and evidence, boundaries, and what happens after. A separate `/evidence` page explains the structure of the problem the company addresses, distinct from the Voices, who validate that the problem is real. The verbatim FAQ is ingested separately in a later phase; here the agent only needs to know the surface exists and what it covers. [LIVE] Source: site map, May 2026.

### The Field Guides index
The Field Guide series is indexed at `/field-guides`. Volume One, "It Starts With Safety," and Volume Two, "It Continues With Exploration," are available; the Skills and Solutions volumes are forthcoming. The Field Guides are the free, self-directed expression of the methodology, and the nav carries a direct link to Volume One. The agent treats the Field Guides as both a product surface and the free path through the first two stages, and confirms current availability against the live index. [LIVE] Source: site map; /pricing copy prompt.

### Contact and intake
The primary contact is josh@movemental.ai. The contact form at `/contact` asks for name, email, organization, role, what kind of organization it is (church, nonprofit, institution, or other), and what is on the person's desk. Interest-tagged contact links route specific intents, such as SafeStart, SandboxLive, Skills, Solutions, the bundle, and institutional inquiries. The stated response time is within one business day, and a first conversation is thirty minutes with Brad, Alan, or Josh. [LIVE] Verify against `/contact`. Source: site map, May 2026.

### The assessment surfaces
Two assessment surfaces exist. The Integrity Diagnostic at `/assess` maps an organization's AI use and returns a narrative read-back. [CONFLICT] Its specifications differ across sources, so the agent verifies question count, time, and dimensions against the live page before stating them; see Part XIII. A narrower Safety Self-Assessment, a shorter automated check, is also referenced. Both are free and serve as the entry point before an organization chooses a path. Source: site map; How Movemental Uses AI inventory.

### Legal and reference pages
The site carries standard legal pages at `/terms`, `/privacy`, and `/cookies`, and a reference page at `/footnotes` that holds the citations behind the statistics used across the site, with individual anchors per claim. These were referenced but not fully captured in the crawl, so the agent points to them rather than quoting their contents, and uses `/footnotes` as the home for evidence behind any site statistic. Source: site map, May 2026.

*Phase 4 of the knowledge base. This completes the four phases that were ready to write from documents in hand. Phase 5 (audiences and playbooks, governance and legal, voice and vocabulary and FAQ) remains blocked until the image-only Playbooks and Participation Agreement are read and the live `/faq`, `/assess`, `/how-we-use-ai`, and `/movement-voice-commitments` pages are checked.*
